title: Documents vs Rows
question: How is MongoDB different from a relational database?
answer: MongoDB stores flexible BSON documents in collections instead of rows in tables. Schemas are enforced by the app or with schema validation, not by fixed columns.
explanation: Great for evolving schemas and hierarchical data.
codeExample: db.users.insertOne({ _id: 1, name: 'Ada', roles: ['admin'] });
bestPractices: Design schemas around access patterns.
commonMistakes: Modelling normalized relational schemas 1:1.
interviewTips: Discuss embedded vs referenced documents.

title: Aggregation Pipeline
question: What is the aggregation pipeline?
answer: A sequence of stages ($match, $group, $project, $lookup, $unwind, ...) that transforms documents step by step, similar to SQL groups + joins.
explanation: Very powerful for analytics and reporting.
bestPractices: Put $match and $project first to reduce data early.
commonMistakes: Missing indexes on $match fields.
interviewTips: Explain $lookup as a left outer join.

title: Indexes
question: How do MongoDB indexes work?
answer: B-tree indexes on any field or embedded field, single or compound, plus specialised types (text, geo, hashed, TTL).
explanation: Use explain() to check index usage.
codeExample: db.users.createIndex({ email: 1 }, { unique: true });
bestPractices: Compound index order matters (ESR rule).
commonMistakes: Too many indexes slowing writes.
interviewTips: Discuss covered queries.

title: Replica Sets & Sharding
question: What's the difference between replica sets and sharding?
answer: Replica sets provide HA/failover by replicating the same data across nodes. Sharding splits data horizontally across shards for scale.
explanation: Production clusters combine both — sharded cluster of replica sets.
codeExample:
sh.enableSharding('shop');
sh.shardCollection('shop.orders', { userId: 'hashed' });
bestPractices: Choose a high-cardinality shard key.
commonMistakes: Choosing an ever-increasing shard key causing hot chunks.
interviewTips: Discuss primary election and write concern.

title: CRUD Operations
question: What are the core CRUD operations in MongoDB?
answer: Create (insertOne/insertMany), Read (find/findOne), Update (updateOne/updateMany/replaceOne), and Delete (deleteOne/deleteMany). Each accepts a query filter plus options.
explanation: CRUD is the foundation of the MongoDB driver and shell API; every higher-level operation builds on these primitives.
bestPractices: Prefer updateOne with $set over replaceOne to avoid losing fields.
Always pass a precise filter to avoid mass updates.
commonMistakes: Using updateMany when updateOne was intended.
Forgetting await and losing errors.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between replaceOne and updateOne.

title: Query Operators
question: What are MongoDB query operators and how are they used?
answer: Query operators like $eq, $gt, $lt, $in, $nin, $and, $or, $exists, and $regex let you express conditions in a find filter beyond simple equality.
explanation: Operators are prefixed with $ and let you build rich predicates over document fields, including nested paths using dot notation.
bestPractices: Use $in instead of chained $or for the same field.
Index fields used in selective filters.
commonMistakes: Confusing $ne/$nin which cannot use indexes efficiently.
interviewTips: Contrast $or with $in for a single field.

title: Update Operators
question: Which update operators does MongoDB provide?
answer: Field operators like $set, $unset, $inc, $mul, $rename, $min, $max, and array operators like $push, $pull, $addToSet, and $pop modify documents in place.
explanation: Update operators mutate specific fields atomically at the document level without needing to read then rewrite the whole document.
bestPractices: Use $addToSet to avoid duplicate array entries.
Prefer $inc over read-modify-write for counters.
commonMistakes: Replacing the whole document instead of using $set.
interviewTips: Explain atomicity at the single-document level.

title: Projection
question: What is projection and how do you limit returned fields?
answer: Projection selects which fields to include (1) or exclude (0) in query results. You cannot mix inclusion and exclusion except for _id.
explanation: Projection reduces network payload and enables covered queries when all requested fields are in an index.
bestPractices: Return only fields the client needs.
Exclude _id explicitly when not required.
commonMistakes: Mixing inclusion and exclusion in one projection.
interviewTips: Connect projection to covered queries.

title: Embedding vs Referencing
question: When should you embed documents versus reference them?
answer: Embed when data is accessed together and has bounded size (1:1 or 1:few). Reference when data is large, shared, or grows unbounded (1:many, many:many).
explanation: Embedding favors read performance and atomic updates; referencing favors normalization and avoids document bloat.
bestPractices: Model around your most frequent query patterns.
Watch the 16MB document size limit.
commonMistakes: Embedding unbounded arrays that grow forever.
interviewTips: Give a concrete 1:few vs 1:many example.

title: Schema Design Patterns
question: What common schema design patterns exist in MongoDB?
answer: Patterns include Bucket, Computed, Subset, Extended Reference, Outlier, Schema Versioning, and Attribute patterns to optimize for access patterns.
explanation: These patterns solve recurring modeling problems like large arrays, expensive aggregations, or evolving schemas.
bestPractices: Apply the Subset pattern to keep hot fields small.
Use Schema Versioning for gradual migrations.
commonMistakes: Over-engineering patterns before measuring need.
interviewTips: Name a pattern and the problem it solves.

title: Data Modeling Relationships
question: How do you model 1:1, 1:N, and N:N relationships?
answer: 1:1 usually embeds. 1:N embeds when N is small/bounded or references when large. N:N uses references, often with an array of IDs on one or both sides or a linking collection.
explanation: Cardinality and access patterns drive whether to embed or reference each relationship.
bestPractices: Store the array on the side queried most.
Keep referenced arrays bounded.
commonMistakes: Duplicating large data on both sides of N:N.
interviewTips: Explain how $lookup resolves references.

title: Multi-Document Transactions
question: How do multi-document transactions work in MongoDB?
answer: Transactions group multiple operations across documents/collections into an atomic unit using a session with startTransaction/commitTransaction, available on replica sets and sharded clusters.
explanation: They provide ACID guarantees across documents but add overhead, so single-document atomicity should be preferred where possible.
bestPractices: Keep transactions short to reduce lock contention.
Prefer document-level atomicity when feasible.
commonMistakes: Using transactions where a single-document update would suffice.
interviewTips: Mention retryable writes and transaction limits.

title: Write Concern & Read Preference
question: What are write concern and read preference?
answer: Write concern (w, j, wtimeout) controls acknowledgement durability of writes. Read preference (primary, secondary, nearest) controls which replica-set members serve reads.
explanation: Together they tune the consistency/durability vs latency/availability trade-off in a replica set.
bestPractices: Use w:'majority' for critical writes.
Read from secondaries only when stale reads are acceptable.
commonMistakes: Assuming secondary reads are always up to date.
interviewTips: Explain the durability meaning of j:true.

title: Aggregation Stages
question: What do $lookup, $group, $unwind, and $facet do?
answer: $lookup joins another collection, $group aggregates by a key, $unwind flattens arrays into multiple documents, and $facet runs multiple sub-pipelines in parallel over the same input.
explanation: These stages compose to build analytics pipelines, combining joins, grouping, array expansion, and multi-faceted results.
bestPractices: Filter with $match before $lookup to reduce joins.
Use $facet to compute multiple summaries in one pass.
commonMistakes: $unwind on huge arrays exploding document counts.
interviewTips: Describe $lookup as a left outer join.

title: Text Search
question: How does text search work in MongoDB?
answer: Create a text index on one or more string fields, then query with $text and $search. Results can be ranked by relevance using the textScore metadata.
explanation: Text indexes tokenize and stem words, enabling language-aware keyword search without an external engine for simple cases.
bestPractices: Only one text index is allowed per collection.
Sort by textScore for relevance ranking.
commonMistakes: Expecting substring matches like SQL LIKE.
interviewTips: Contrast text search with $regex.

title: Geospatial Queries
question: How do you run geospatial queries in MongoDB?
answer: Use a 2dsphere index on GeoJSON fields and operators like $near, $geoWithin, and $geoIntersects to query by proximity or containment.
explanation: MongoDB supports spherical geometry, enabling 'find places within X meters' style queries efficiently.
bestPractices: Store coordinates as [longitude, latitude].
Use 2dsphere for real-world earth geometry.
commonMistakes: Swapping latitude and longitude order.
interviewTips: Explain $near requires a geospatial index.

title: TTL Indexes
question: What is a TTL index and when is it useful?
answer: A TTL (time-to-live) index automatically deletes documents after a set number of seconds based on a date field, ideal for sessions, logs, and caches.
explanation: A background thread periodically removes expired documents, so cleanup is automatic without cron jobs.
bestPractices: Ensure the indexed field stores a BSON Date.
Expect deletion within a minute of expiry, not instant.
commonMistakes: Using a non-date field which disables expiry.
interviewTips: Note TTL is not exact real-time deletion.

title: Compound & Multikey Indexes
question: What are compound and multikey indexes?
answer: A compound index covers multiple fields in a defined order and follows the ESR (Equality, Sort, Range) rule. A multikey index is created automatically when a field holds an array, indexing each element.
explanation: Compound index field order determines which queries it can serve; multikey indexes let array-element queries use an index.
bestPractices: Order compound keys by the ESR rule.
Avoid compounding two array fields (not allowed).
commonMistakes: Wrong field order making the index unusable for a query.
interviewTips: Explain the ESR rule with an example.

title: Covered Queries & $regex
question: What is a covered query and how does $regex affect performance?
answer: A covered query is served entirely from an index without reading documents, requiring all queried and projected fields to be in the index. Anchored $regex (^prefix) can use an index; unanchored or case-insensitive regex usually cannot.
explanation: Covered queries are fast because they skip document fetches; poorly written regex triggers full collection scans.
bestPractices: Anchor regex with ^ to leverage indexes.
Include all needed fields in the index for coverage.
commonMistakes: Case-insensitive regex causing full scans.
interviewTips: Verify coverage with explain() totalDocsExamined: 0.

title: Pagination, Upsert & Change Streams
question: How do you paginate results, use upsert, and consume change streams?
answer: Prefer range-based (keyset) pagination over skip/limit for large sets. Upsert inserts when no match is found via the upsert:true option. Change streams let apps subscribe to real-time data changes via watch().
explanation: Keyset pagination avoids the O(n) cost of large skips; upsert simplifies insert-or-update; change streams enable event-driven reactions to writes.
bestPractices: Use keyset pagination for deep pages.
Use upsert to make writes idempotent.
commonMistakes: Relying on skip() for large offsets.
interviewTips: Explain why skip/limit degrades on deep pages.
